description: Completely rework the voice of the 4 Getting Started docs pages (index, quick-start, first-plugin, install) to match the personality, honesty, humor, and genuine warmth of the README. The current pages have first-person pronouns but still read like polished technical documentation. They need to sound like a real person wrote them — the same person who wrote the README.
qualityChecks: cd docs && npm run build && npm run type-check && npm run lint && npm run knip && npm run format:check

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/index.mdx voice to match README personality
userStories.description: The docs index page is the first thing people read after the README. It currently reads like documentation with 'I' inserted. It needs to match the README's energy — genuine excitement, honest tradeoffs, light humor, personality in every paragraph. Study the README at the repo root as the gold standard.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening lines have the same punch and specificity as the README — mention reverse-engineering internal APIs, the (okay, Claude did it) parenthetical energy, 'tokens are money' directness
The 'How It Works' section explains WHY the Chrome extension is needed (sessions live in the browser) with the same clarity as the README's 'Those internal APIs need to run inside your browser'
Key Features reads as genuine statements about what's actually good, not a marketing bullet list — each bullet should feel like something the author is personally proud of or thinks matters
There is an honest recommendation to build plugins instead of installing third-party ones, matching the README's 'My honest recommendation' section about trust
No corporate language — no 'leverage', 'empower', 'solution', 'ecosystem'
The page flows naturally from idea → how it works → why plugins → quick start, not as disconnected sections
All MDX components (<HowItWorks />) preserved exactly
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/index.mdx (complete voice rework)

Context:
The README (at repo root README.md) is the gold standard for voice. Key passages to study and channel:

1. README opening: "I reverse-engineered (okay, I asked Claude Opus to reverse-engineer) the internal APIs that web apps use — the same endpoints their own frontend calls — and exposed them as MCP tools so your AI agent can call them directly."
2. README punch: "No screenshots. No DOM scraping. No pixel-guessing. When your AI wants to send a Slack message, it calls slack_send_message — the exact same API call Slack's own web app makes, running in your browser, using your existing session. It's fast, it's cheap on tokens (tokens are money), and the knowledge gets packaged into a reusable plugin anyone can install."
3. README honest recommendation: "My honest recommendation: build your own plugins instead of installing third-party ones. Plugins run in your browser with your logged-in sessions — you should know what the code does."
4. README How It Works: "Those internal APIs need to run inside your browser — that's where your sessions live."

Current index.mdx opening: "Your AI agent can interact with any web app through your logged-in browser session. That's the core idea." — This is BLAND compared to the README. It needs the same specificity and personality.

The current page structure is:
- Intro (4 paragraphs)
- Who Is This For? (3 audience links)
- How It Works (illustration + 3 numbered steps)
- Key Features (5 bullets)
- Next Steps (1 link)

The new page should have a similar structure but with:
- An intro that LEADS with the idea (reverse-engineering internal APIs) with README-level energy
- An honest recommendation about building vs installing plugins (from README)
- Key features that feel genuine, not like a feature checklist
- Mention of plugin count and variety ("over a hundred plugins" — messaging, project management, etc.) like the README does
- Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, OpenCode — name them like the README does

Constraints:
- Keep <HowItWorks /> component exactly where it is
- Keep all links exactly as they are
- Keep frontmatter structure (title, description, lastUpdated)
- Do NOT copy the README verbatim — the docs should be the docs, not a README duplicate. But channel the same ENERGY and PERSONALITY.
- Do NOT add emojis

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/quick-start.mdx voice to match README personality
userStories.description: The quick-start page currently reads like polished getting-started docs. It should sound like someone personally walking you through setup — honest about the rough edges (loading unpacked extensions isn't ideal), excited about the result.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening feels personal and direct, not like a product onboarding flow
The extension loading step acknowledges it's a bit manual (like the README's straightforward tone)
The MCP client config step feels like 'here grab this and paste it' not like a formal configuration procedure
The plugin install step includes the honest recommendation about building your own (or at least links to it)
The 'something looks off' section feels genuinely helpful — 'open an issue and I'll take a look'
All code blocks, MDX components (<QuickStartFlow />), and links preserved exactly
No corporate language
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/quick-start.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
Current opening: "Let me get you up and running. Six steps, about 5 minutes."
This is okay but still feels like documentation. Compare to how the README handles setup: "You need Node.js 22+ and Chrome." then just shows the commands. Direct, no ceremony.

The README's setup section is punchy: commands first, explanations where needed, "That's it" at the end. The quick-start should channel that energy.

Current structure (keep this):
1. Install the CLI
2. Start the server
3. Load the Chrome extension
4. Configure your MCP client
5. Install a plugin
6. Make a tool call

The prose between steps needs personality. For example:
- Step 3 (load extension): could acknowledge "Yeah, you have to manually load it. Chrome doesn't let you distribute MV3 extensions outside the Chrome Web Store for localhost tools. It takes 30 seconds."
- Step 5 (install plugin): could echo the README's honest recommendation about building your own
- The doctor/status section at the end is good but could have more personality

Constraints:
- Keep <QuickStartFlow /> component
- Keep all code blocks exactly
- Keep all links exactly
- Do NOT copy README text — channel the energy

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/first-plugin.mdx voice to match README personality
userStories.description: The first-plugin page is where the author's genuine excitement about plugin building should come through most strongly. The README says 'This is the part I'm most excited about' — that energy should be here too.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening conveys genuine excitement about building plugins, matching the README's 'This is the part I'm most excited about'
The 'document and window are the real thing' note is highlighted with personality — this is genuinely cool and should feel that way
The 'What just happened?' section feels like someone personally walking you through the magic, not a technical recap
There's an honest note about the trust model — you built it, you can read every line
All code blocks, MDX components (<FirstPluginFlow />), and links preserved exactly
No corporate language
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/first-plugin.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
README's plugin section: "This is the part I'm most excited about. Point your AI agent at any website and it'll build a plugin for you... Your agent wrote it, you can read every line, and it runs in your browser with your session. That's a pretty good trust model."

Current page opening: "Let me walk you through building your first plugin. By the end, you'll have a tool that your AI agent can call through your browser — and you'll understand how all the pieces fit together."
This is fine but generic. It should have the README's excitement.

The page structure (keep this):
1. Scaffold the project
2. Define a tool
3. Register the tool
4. Build
5. Test it
Then: What just happened?

Key opportunities for personality:
- The tool code uses `document.title` and `window.location.href` — this is running IN the page, same as DevTools. That's genuinely cool.
- The 'What just happened?' chain (build → register → server → extension → tab → result) is the whole platform in 5 lines. Make it feel like a reveal.
- Could mention that most plugins in the repo were built by AI in minutes (from README)
- Could mention the build-plugin skill that teaches AI to build plugins (from README)

Constraints:
- Keep <FirstPluginFlow /> component
- Keep all code blocks exactly
- Keep all links exactly

userStories.title: Rework docs/content/docs/install/index.mdx voice to match README personality
userStories.description: The installation page has three paths (user, plugin dev, contributor). It currently reads like a setup guide. It should feel like someone personally telling you what to do based on why you're here.
acceptanceCriteria: Opening is direct and personal — not 'I've split installation into three paths' (which is about the docs structure) but about what you're here to do
The 'For Users' section has the README's directness — commands first, explanations where needed
The 'For Plugin Developers' section conveys that building plugins is the recommended path with genuine enthusiasm
The 'For Contributors' section feels welcoming — 'Welcome aboard' or similar
The rebuild/reload section for plugin devs has personality about the feedback loop being fast
All MDX components (<InstallPaths />, <CliCommand />), code blocks, and links preserved exactly
No corporate language
userStories.notes:
Files:
- docs/content/docs/install/index.mdx (voice rework)

Context:
Current opening: "I've split installation into three paths depending on what you're here to do. Pick the one that fits and skip the rest."
This is about the page structure, not about the reader. Compare to README: "You need Node.js 22+ and Chrome." — just gets into it.

The page has three sections:
1. For Users — install CLI, start server, load extension, configure MCP client, install plugins, verify
2. For Plugin Developers — user setup + scaffold + build/register + rebuild/reload
3. For Contributors — clone + build + extension + dev server + MCP client + checks

Key opportunities:
- The plugin developer section should echo the README's enthusiasm about building plugins
- The feedback loop note ("I wanted the feedback loop to be tight") is good but could be stronger
- The contributor section's "Welcome aboard" is good
- Could be more honest about the extension loading being manual

Constraints:
- Keep <InstallPaths /> and <CliCommand /> components exactly
- Keep all code blocks
- Keep all links
